notes: Entity confirmed: stored URL resolves to 'American jazz pianist and composer (1917-1982)'. Verified: birth/death years, piano/composer, angular melodies, dissonances, percussive attack, whole-tone runs, silences, ''Round Midnight'/'Straight, No Chaser'/'Blue Monk' as repertoire standards, house pianist at Minton's Playhouse where bebop was formulated. Unverifiable (extract truncates around 1948): the 'wrongful cabaret card revocation', 'major recognition in the late 1950s with Riverside and Columbia recordings', and influence on avant-garde/art music. No contradictions found.
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Birth (June 7, 1932), death (August 13, 1974), tenor saxophone, hard bop association all confirmed by the stored-URL page.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Born June 16, 1946 (living, deathYear null correct); trumpet/flugelhorn confirmed. All award claims (JJA Trumpeter of the Year 2018, DownBeat awards, SESAC, BMI, Prix Oscar du Jazz, Grammy nomination for Time's Mirror) appear word-for-word in the evidence.

Verified: 1942-2018, Polish trumpeter, free jazz association, Komeda connection (joined Komeda quintet 1963, recorded on Astigmatic), ECM recordings, late-career quartet of young Polish musicians (Wasilewski/Kurkiewicz/16-year-old Miskiewicz) plus the 2007 Scandinavian quintet (Dark Eyes, ECM), international standing. 'Dark, haunting sound' and 'melancholy of Eastern European music' are editorial, not flagged.

Verified: Anthony Tillmon Williams, 1945-1997, drummer, joined Miles Davis's Second Great Quintet at 17, pioneered fusion with The Tony Williams Lifetime, polyrhythms. 'One of the most innovative drummers' / 'influenced generations' are editorial, consistent with hall-of-fame inductions and the Christgau quote in evidence.

Verified: 1935-1973, British, tenor saxophone + flute + vibraphone (all three confirmed in evidence), died at 38. Unverifiable: 'led his own band at 16' (evidence says at 16 he JOINED Kenny Baker's sextet; his own octet was formed in 1955 - in tension but not contradicted); 'first British jazz musician to lead his own group at a New York club' (evidence describes the Half Note residency via a Musicians' Union exchange but never says 'first'); 'heart problems' as cause of death (extract truncates before his death). 'Greatest jazz musician Britain ever produced' is an editorial superlative; evidence says 'one of the finest jazz saxophonists to have emerged from Britain'.

Verified: born 1980, MacArthur Fellowship 2017, drums/trombone (began as classical trombone major)/piano/composer, album 'The Inner Spectrum of Variables' exists (2016, trio plus strings), work spans jazz and classical from solo percussion to opera and orchestral commissions. Editorial characterizations consistent with evidence.

Catalog placeholder, not a real artist. The bio's only claim ('Compilation or collection featuring multiple contributing artists') matches the resolved page - 'Various artists' redirects to 'Compilation album', which covers exactly this concept. birthYear 0 is a placeholder artifact a repair-writer may want to null; empty instruments list is expected. Nothing contradicted.

Bio is near-verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Born September 11, 1964; Flecktones bassist since the group's 1988 formation; SMV with Stanley Clarke and Marcus Miller (evidence adds 2008-2009); Nitro 2017-2019. All confirmed.

Bio is an older snapshot of the Wikipedia lede; born October 26, 1971, pianist, NYT quote, MacArthur 2013, Doris Duke, USA Fellowship, Alpert Award all verbatim-confirmed. One staleness: ours says 'a Grammy nomination' where the current page says 'three Grammy nominations' - a subset, not a contradiction, so not flagged as error; repair-writer may update the count.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede; born February 5, 1956; Modern Drummer HoF 1996, Classic Drummer HoF 2014, one Grammy + two nominations, Zappa/Mitchell/Sting all confirmed word-for-word. Data-quality (not a factual error): instruments field contains 'musician' rather than an instrument; evidence identifies him as a drummer - should be 'drums'.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede, including dates (May 25, 1960 - March 31, 2020), one Grammy + two nominations, lessons from Clark Terry and Dizzy Gillespie, study with Miles Davis 1985-1991, and the 'only trumpet player Davis personally mentored' line. All confirmed.

Verified: 1933-2023, tenor/soprano saxophone + composer, Jazz Messengers from 1959 staying four years (= ours '1959-63'), joined Davis's Second Great Quintet 1964 with last Davis recordings 1970 (= ours '1964-70'), Blue Note leader albums Night Dreamer/JuJu/Speak No Evil, Weather Report co-founder 1970, compositions became enduring standards. 'Creatively vital into his 80s' consistent with the six-decade career and death at 89 in 2023.

Verified: active 1970-1986 (matches birthYear/deathYear), founded 1970, co-led by Zawinul and Shorter (both played on Davis's In a Silent Way / Bitches Brew, supporting 'formed from Miles Davis alumni'), evolution from free-improvising/experimental beginnings to a funky groove-oriented sound, 'Birdland' as 1977 hit single, Jaco Pastorius's virtuoso impact, 16-year career, fusion-defining status. Unverifiable: the album title 'Heavy Weather' (1977) - the extract confirms 'Birdland' as a 1977 hit single but never names its parent album. No contradictions. Minor: the page lede says active to 1986 while the body mentions 'break-up in late 1985'; ours matches the lede.
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. 1923-1968, guitarist, thumb-plucking technique and octaves all confirmed.
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede (full name, 'the Lion' nickname, American jazz and stride pianist). 1893-1973 confirmed (November 23, 1893 - April 18, 1973).
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. 1944-1989, trumpet/flugelhorn/cornet, composer-arranger-bandleader-educator framing all confirmed word-for-word.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: The World Saxophone Quartet was an American jazz ensemble founded in 1977
The ensemble had split up in 2016.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Founded in 1976, the group proved that four saxophones could generate the full range of jazz expression
wrongClaims.problem: Founding year contradicts the entity-matched Wikipedia page, which dates the founding to 1977
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: The World Saxophone Quartet was an American jazz ensemble founded in 1977

notes: Right entity: original membership (Julius Hemphill, Oliver Lake, David Murray, Hamiet Bluiett) confirmed exactly. 'Performing without a rhythm section' is consistent with 'principally recorded and performed as a saxophone quartet' (later sometimes joined by drummers/bassists). The 'Revue' album is unverifiable - the discography is not in the extract. Caveat for human review: founding is sometimes dated late 1976 (first concerts) vs 1977 elsewhere, so the 1976-vs-1977 discrepancy may be a genuine source ambiguity rather than corruption; the evidence as given says 1977. Bio's present tense ('is a groundbreaking ensemble') also conflicts with the 2016 split and 'was' in evidence.

Verified: born 1961, trumpeter/composer/educator, moved to New York in 1979 (Juilliard), Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers 1980-82, own quintets, Blood on the Fields as first jazz Pulitzer (1997), Jazz at Lincoln Center artistic director (helped start the 1987 Classical Jazz series that became J@LC), education/media work (Marsalis on Music, Peabody, CBS), and criticism of his conservative aesthetic consistent with the page's 'Debate on jazz' section. 'Led the neo-traditional movement' and 'jazz's first major permanent institution' are editorial characterizations, not contradicted.

The stored ja.wikipedia URL (titled with the Japanese name) returned no page (page: null) - the article may not exist or the title may be wrong, so the site may be shipping a dead link; flag for link check. No evidence document survived, so nothing is checkable: birthYear 1965, the Bill Evans influence, and 'His Village Vanguard appearance marked a milestone for Japanese jazz' are all unverified.

Verified: 1920-2013, tenor saxophone and flute as main instruments plus oboe (rare in jazz), bamboo flute and shehnai (ours 'shanai'), 'played world music before world music had a name', Eastern Sounds (1961), conversion to Islam (Ahmadiyya) in the period after his 1950 return to Detroit (ours 'in 1950' consistent), doctorate (Ed.D. 1975, UMass Amherst). 'Writing extensively on music education' is loosely supported (flute method book, education dissertation, professorships) and not contradicted.

Verified: 1959-2011, South African saxophonist/flautist (+ composer per short description), 'Zimology' attested ('He was later known as Zimology'; album and institute of that name). Unverifiable: 'A student of Bheki Mseleku' - evidence names Archie Shepp and Yusef Lateef as his teachers (UMass) and never mentions Mseleku (absence, not contradiction); 'Xhosa musical traditions' and 'drew equally on free jazz and indigenous African music' are not attested in evidence. Nuance: ours says he called his STYLE Zimology; evidence presents Zimology as a name he was known by.

Opensearch top result clearly matches our entity (1903-1931, American jazz cornetist, pianist and composer); ours' years and instruments are all confirmed. The stub bio's only factual content (jazz, cornet, piano) is consistent. Data-quality for repair-writer: bio is a malformed auto-generated stub ('Bix Beiderbecke was a jazz cornet, piano.'); instruments array holds one comma-joined string 'cornet, piano' instead of separate entries; no wikipediaUrl stored although the correct page exists. Note: this is the artist whose ALBUM entry was a known disease example ('Singing the Blues' pop-song link) - no such contamination in this artist record.
